title: CDMGRestrictions
lean_code_with_comments:
theorem no_arrowhead_into_J (G : CDMG Node) {j : Node} (hj : j ∈ G.J)
    {v : Node} (hv : v ∈ G.J ∪ G.V) :
    ¬ G.hus j v
-- claim_3_1 -- end statement
  := by
    -- `hv` is part of the statement for LN-faithful quantifier range
    -- (`v ∈ J ∪ V`), but the contradiction below uses only `hj` and the
    -- typing constraints — the proof faithfully mirrors the tex proof,
    -- which also "fixes $v$" without consuming it.
    let _ := hv
    intro h
    unfold CDMG.hus CDMG.hut CDMG.huh at h
    rcases h with h | h
    · obtain ⟨_, hjV⟩ := G.hE_subset h
      exact Finset.disjoint_left.mp G.hJV_disj hj hjV
    · obtain ⟨hjV, _⟩ := G.hL_subset h
      exact Finset.disjoint_left.mp G.hJV_disj hj hjV

theorem J_to_V_edge_admissible (G : CDMG Node) {j : Node} (hj : j ∈ G.J)
    {v : Node} (hv : v ∈ G.V) :
    j ∈ G.J ∪ G.V ∧ v ∈ G.V
-- claim_3_1 -- end statement
  := by exact ⟨Finset.mem_union_left _ hj, hv⟩

theorem J_nodes_not_adjacent (G : CDMG Node) {j₁ : Node} (hj₁ : j₁ ∈ G.J)
    {j₂ : Node} (hj₂ : j₂ ∈ G.J) :
    ¬ G.adjacent j₁ j₂
-- claim_3_1 -- end statement
  := by
    intro h
    unfold CDMG.adjacent CDMG.sus CDMG.tuh CDMG.hut CDMG.huh at h
    rcases h with h | h | h
    · obtain ⟨_, hj₂V⟩ := G.hE_subset h
      exact Finset.disjoint_left.mp G.hJV_disj hj₂ hj₂V
    · obtain ⟨_, hj₁V⟩ := G.hE_subset h
      exact Finset.disjoint_left.mp G.hJV_disj hj₁ hj₁V
    · obtain ⟨hj₁V, _⟩ := G.hL_subset h
      exact Finset.disjoint_left.mp G.hJV_disj hj₁ hj₁V
lean_code_without_comments:
theorem no_arrowhead_into_J (G : CDMG Node) {j : Node} (hj : j ∈ G.J)
    {v : Node} (hv : v ∈ G.J ∪ G.V) :
    ¬ G.hus j v

  := by

    let _ := hv
    intro h
    unfold CDMG.hus CDMG.hut CDMG.huh at h
    rcases h with h | h
    · obtain ⟨_, hjV⟩ := G.hE_subset h
      exact Finset.disjoint_left.mp G.hJV_disj hj hjV
    · obtain ⟨hjV, _⟩ := G.hL_subset h
      exact Finset.disjoint_left.mp G.hJV_disj hj hjV

theorem J_to_V_edge_admissible (G : CDMG Node) {j : Node} (hj : j ∈ G.J)
    {v : Node} (hv : v ∈ G.V) :
    j ∈ G.J ∪ G.V ∧ v ∈ G.V

  := by exact ⟨Finset.mem_union_left _ hj, hv⟩

theorem J_nodes_not_adjacent (G : CDMG Node) {j₁ : Node} (hj₁ : j₁ ∈ G.J)
    {j₂ : Node} (hj₂ : j₂ ∈ G.J) :
    ¬ G.adjacent j₁ j₂

  := by
    intro h
    unfold CDMG.adjacent CDMG.sus CDMG.tuh CDMG.hut CDMG.huh at h
    rcases h with h | h | h
    · obtain ⟨_, hj₂V⟩ := G.hE_subset h
      exact Finset.disjoint_left.mp G.hJV_disj hj₂ hj₂V
    · obtain ⟨_, hj₁V⟩ := G.hE_subset h
      exact Finset.disjoint_left.mp G.hJV_disj hj₁ hj₁V
    · obtain ⟨hj₁V, _⟩ := G.hL_subset h
      exact Finset.disjoint_left.mp G.hJV_disj hj₁ hj₁V
lean_explanation:
The LN remark `CDMGRestrictions` collects three consequences of the typing constraints carried by a CDMG (def_3_1): $E \subseteq (J \cup V) \times V$, $L \subseteq V \times V$, and $J \cap V = \emptyset$. Each consequence is formalised as its own `theorem` rather than packing all three into a single conjunction, so downstream consumers can invoke them individually and so the LN's distinct quantifier ranges for the three clauses are preserved.

All three theorems share the same statement context, declared once via a `variable` block: `{Node : Type*} [DecidableEq Node]`. Here `Node` is the polymorphic ambient type of graph vertices — matching the chapter convention so later chapters (CBNs, do-calculus, iSCMs) can instantiate at the carrier their random variables live on — and `[DecidableEq Node]` is the minimal typeclass inherited from def_3_1 (it is needed for `Disjoint J V`, for `Finset` membership tests, and for unfolding `G.hus` into a disjunction of `Finset`-memberships). Each theorem also takes `G : CDMG Node` explicitly, which enables the dot-notation `G.no_arrowhead_into_J hj hv` that mirrors the LN's left-to-right reading `j \hus v$ in $G$.

The three theorems are: `no_arrowhead_into_J`, which takes `j ∈ G.J` and `v ∈ G.J ∪ G.V` and concludes `¬ G.hus j v` — a literal transliteration of the LN's `$j \hus v \notin G$` via the named predicate `G.hus` from `CDMGNotation.lean` (def_3_2 item 6); `J_to_V_edge_admissible`, which takes `j ∈ G.J` and `v ∈ G.V` and concludes `j ∈ G.J ∪ G.V ∧ v ∈ G.V` — exactly the RHS of `G.hE_subset`'s implication, instantiated at the pair `(j, v)`; and `J_nodes_not_adjacent`, which takes `j₁, j₂ ∈ G.J` and concludes `¬ G.adjacent j₁ j₂`, with `G.adjacent` being def_3_3 item i (the three-way disjunction `tuh ∨ hut ∨ huh`).

Notable Mathlib idioms in play: `Finset Node` and `Finset (Node × Node)` for the four CDMG components, `Disjoint G.J G.V` for the LN's $J \cap V = \emptyset$ (unpacked via `Finset.disjoint_left.mp`), and `Finset.mem_union_left` to inject `G.J ↪ G.J ∪ G.V`. Implicit binders `{j}`, `{v}`, `{j₁}`, `{j₂}` follow standard Mathlib style: variables appearing in subsequent argument types are made implicit so callers only supply the membership hypotheses and let Lean infer the witnesses.
design_choices:
**Three separate theorems, not one conjunction.** The LN enumerates three numbered consequences, each with its own use downstream — walks (def_3_4) need (i) to rule out walks ending at a `J`-node by a head, family relationships (def_3_5) use (i) and (iii) to make $\mathrm{Pa}^G(j) = \emptyset$ for $j \in J$, and hard intervention (def_3_10) leverages (ii) when it converts $V$-nodes into $J$-nodes. A single conjunction `∀ j v, P₁ ∧ P₂ ∧ P₃` would force every consumer to destructure even when only one consequence is wanted, and the three clauses have different quantifier ranges (clauses (i) and (iii) bind two variables, clause (ii) is a permission claim with `v ∈ V` instead of `v ∈ J ∪ V`).

**Conclusions use named predicates `G.hus` and `G.adjacent`, not their unfolded set-theoretic forms.** The LN writes clauses (i) and (iii) as `$j \hus v \notin G$` and `not adjacent in $G$ in the sense of def_3_3 item~i.` respectively; `CDMGNotation.lean` and `EdgeRelations.lean` exist precisely so these LN macros have a 1:1 Lean form. Stating the conclusions as the unfolded conjunction (e.g. `(v, j) ∉ G.E ∧ (j, v) ∉ G.L`) would mirror the canonical tex's expansion sentence but force every downstream consumer to re-derive the disjunction-to-conjunction step instead of `unfold G.hus` once. Faithfulness to the LN macro wins; downstream proofs can always unfold on demand.

**Clause (ii) is encoded as the typing-constraint precondition, not as an extensibility statement.** The natural reading of "the type does not forbid an edge $j \tuh v$" is the existence claim `∃ G' : CDMG Node, G'.E = G.E ∪ {(j, v)} ∧ <still valid>`. That form needs witness construction and re-proof of every typing field. The rewritten canonical tex chose the lighter form: the literal pair $(j, v)$ satisfies the RHS of `hE_subset`, i.e. `j ∈ G.J ∪ G.V ∧ v ∈ G.V`. Downstream consumers that need the extensibility version can derive it from this theorem.

**No `j₁ ≠ j₂` precondition in (iii).** The LN says "no two nodes in $J$ are adjacent" without restricting to distinct nodes. The diagonal case `j₁ = j₂` is in fact covered: the $E$-branches force `j ∈ V` (contradicting `Disjoint G.J G.V`), and the $L$-branch additionally contradicts `hL_irrefl`. Stating the theorem without the distinctness hypothesis stays close to the LN and yields a slightly stronger result for free.

**Implicit binders for the node variables, explicit for `G`.** Standard Mathlib style: variables appearing in subsequent argument types are made implicit so callers supply only the membership hypotheses. `G` is explicit so dot-notation `G.no_arrowhead_into_J hj hv` works at the call site, matching `G.hus`, `G.adjacent`, `G.hE_subset` style throughout the section.
